summary.executiveSummary: The registry contract anchors smart-contract audit records on-chain but provides zero access control: any address may write any audit result for any contract hash, making the entire trust model trivially bypassable. A front-running DoS vector allows an adversary to permanently block legitimate audit records for any target hash. Combined with unvalidated input fields and no revocation path, the contract cannot reliably serve its stated purpose of providing trustworthy on-chain audit attestations for a payments/wallet context.

findings.title: Permissionless anchor_audit — anyone can fabricate audit results
findings.description: anchor_audit is a public entry function with no capability gate, no allowlist, and no admin check. Any account on the network can call it to write an AuditRecord with an arbitrary overall_risk value, arbitrary blob ID, and any contract hash. There is no way to distinguish a record written by the legitimate auditor from one written by an attacker.
findings.impact: An attacker can anchor a fraudulent 'CLEAN' (overall_risk=0) record for any malicious payment contract before the real auditor does, causing downstream consumers of verify_audit to trust the malicious contract. Conversely, they can mark legitimate contracts as 'CRITICAL' (4) to suppress usage. In a payments and wallet context this directly enables financial loss.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a capability object (e.g., AuditorCap has key, store) minted only by init and transferred to the trusted auditor address. Require this capability as an argument to anchor_audit. Alternatively gate on an allowlist of approved auditor addresses stored in the registry and controlled by an AdminCap.

findings.title: Front-running DoS permanently blocks legitimate audit records
findings.description: table::add aborts the transaction if the key already exists. Because anchor_audit is permissionless (FIND-001), an adversary monitoring the mempool can observe a pending legitimate anchor transaction, extract the contract_hash, and submit their own anchor_audit for the same hash with a higher gas price. Once their record is committed, every subsequent attempt by the legitimate auditor for that hash will abort permanently — the record can never be overwritten or replaced.
findings.impact: Legitimate audits for targeted contracts can be blocked indefinitely at low cost. The attacker's fraudulent record (any risk value, any blob ID) becomes the permanent canonical record. Critical security findings could be suppressed by anchoring a fake CLEAN record before the real audit completes.
findings.recommendation: Either (1) use table::upsert semantics (add if absent, overwrite if present) restricted to the capability holder, or (2) store a vector of AuditRecord per hash to support versioned audits, with the latest record authoritative. At minimum, ensure only credentialed auditors can write, which reduces but does not eliminate the race if multiple auditors exist.

findings.title: overall_risk accepts out-of-range values (>4)
findings.description: The overall_risk field is documented as 0=Clean through 4=Critical, but no assertion enforces this range. A u8 can hold 0–255. Any caller can pass overall_risk=200 or any other undefined value. Consumers reading the stored record or event and expecting values 0–4 will encounter undefined behaviour in their off-chain and on-chain logic.
findings.impact: Off-chain dashboards and on-chain integrations that switch on overall_risk may produce incorrect output or panic. An attacker could use an out-of-range value as a signal to confuse downstream aggregators or hide a record from normal queries.
findings.recommendation: Add: assert!(overall_risk <= 4, EInvalidRiskLevel); before writing the record. Define a named error constant for the abort code.

findings.title: Unbounded input vectors enable gas griefing and storage bloat
findings.description: contract_hash and walrus_blob_id are accepted as vector<u8> with no length validation. A caller can pass vectors of arbitrary length (megabytes). Every byte is charged to the caller in gas, but the storage cost is charged to the shared object's storage rebate pool and ultimately to the ecosystem. With no access control barrier, anyone can bloat the shared registry with garbage data.
findings.impact: Shared object storage bloat degrades performance for all users. In combination with the permissionless write (FIND-001), this is a practical DoS vector against the registry's usability and increases on-chain storage costs.
findings.recommendation: Enforce maximum lengths: assert!(vector::length(&contract_hash) == 32, EInvalidHash); (SHA-256 is exactly 32 bytes). Define an appropriate maximum for walrus_blob_id based on the Walrus spec and assert it. Reject empty vectors as well.

findings.title: No audit update or revocation mechanism
findings.description: Once an AuditRecord is anchored, the contract provides no function to update, supersede, or revoke it. If a new vulnerability is discovered in an audited contract after anchoring, or if an audit was anchored in error, the on-chain record permanently shows the old risk level. verify_audit returns true for all anchored contracts regardless of whether the audit is current.
findings.impact: Stale or incorrect audit records remain authoritative on-chain indefinitely, potentially causing users to trust contracts that have since been found vulnerable. In a payments context this directly endangers user funds.
findings.recommendation: Add an update_audit entry function gated by AuditorCap that uses table::remove followed by table::add (or a direct field mutation). Consider versioning records with an updated_at timestamp and storing the full audit history. verify_audit should optionally return the record details so consumers can check audited_at freshness.

findings.title: AuditAnchored event omits auditor_address
findings.description: The AuditRecord struct stores auditor_address but the AuditAnchored event does not include it. Off-chain indexers relying solely on events to reconstruct audit history cannot attribute records to specific auditors without an additional table lookup or by parsing transaction sender separately.
findings.impact: Reduced auditability and accountability. Fraud detection (who anchored a malicious record) requires cross-referencing event streams with transaction metadata rather than the event alone.
findings.recommendation: Add auditor_address: address to the AuditAnchored struct and populate it from ctx sender in anchor_audit.

findings.title: walrus_blob_id not verified to reference an existing blob
findings.description: The walrus_blob_id field is accepted as an opaque byte vector with no on-chain verification that the blob exists or that its content matches the claimed contract_hash and overall_risk. An auditor or attacker can anchor a record pointing to a non-existent blob, an unrelated blob, or a blob whose content contradicts the on-chain risk score.
findings.impact: The on-chain record and off-chain audit report can be decoupled. A malicious actor could anchor a CLEAN risk score while the referenced blob (if it exists) contains a CRITICAL finding, or point to a blob containing false content entirely.
findings.recommendation: At minimum, document the expected encoding of walrus_blob_id and validate its byte length. If Walrus provides an on-chain object reference for blobs, require that object reference be passed and validated. Consider including a content hash of the blob in the AuditRecord so consumers can verify integrity without trusting the blob store.

findings.title: contract_hash key not validated as canonical hash format
findings.description: contract_hash is used as the table key and is semantically intended to be a deterministic hash of the audited contract bytecode, but no length or format check is enforced. Callers can supply empty vectors, single-byte values, or values of arbitrary length. Two different callers could anchor records for 'the same contract' using different hash representations (e.g., hex-encoded string bytes vs. raw bytes) producing separate table entries.
findings.impact: verify_audit can return false for a legitimately audited contract if the caller used a different encoding. Registry fragmentation reduces the reliability of audit lookups for payment infrastructure consumers.
findings.recommendation: Enforce assert!(vector::length(&contract_hash) == 32, EInvalidHash) for SHA-256, or the appropriate fixed length for the chosen hash algorithm. Document and enforce the canonical encoding (raw bytes, not hex string) in both the contract and the off-chain tooling.

expensivePatterns: Unbounded vector<u8> inputs (contract_hash, walrus_blob_id) stored in a shared Table — each byte costs storage gas with no upper bound enforced on-chain.
table::add on a shared object requires a full object lock on AuditRegistry, serializing all anchor_audit calls globally and increasing contention cost under load.
AuditRecord has copy ability — the entire struct is copied on every table read/event emit, doubling memory pressure for large blob IDs.
optimizationSuggestions: Cap vector inputs to fixed lengths (32 bytes for hash, defined max for blob ID) to make storage cost predictable and bounded.
Consider partitioning the registry into multiple shared objects (e.g., sharded by hash prefix) to reduce write contention on the single AuditRegistry object.
Remove the copy ability from AuditRecord if full-struct copies are not required — use references for reads to avoid unnecessary heap allocation.
Cache total_audits increment: the current pattern (read + increment + write) on a shared object is a known Sui contention hotspot; consider a separate counter object or emit count from events.

overallRecommendations: CRITICAL FIRST: Gate anchor_audit behind a capability object (AuditorCap) created in init and held by the trusted auditor — this single change eliminates FIND-001 and FIND-002 simultaneously and is a prerequisite for the contract being safe to deploy.
Add input validation (hash length == 32, risk value <= 4, blob ID length bounded) before writing any record to prevent malformed data and gas griefing (FIND-003, FIND-004, FIND-008).

Implement an update_audit function gated by AuditorCap to allow correction of stale or erroneous records — immutability without an admin override is dangerous for a trust-critical registry (FIND-005).
Add auditor_address to the AuditAnchored event to enable complete audit attribution from event streams alone without additional RPC calls (FIND-006).

Define and enforce a canonical walrus_blob_id format and document the expected relationship between the blob content and the on-chain risk score, ideally storing a blob content hash for integrity verification (FIND-007).
